perturbation.original_text: that the Recipient shall not disclose Confidential Information to any person other than the Directors, Officers, or Staff Members of the Association, unless he/she has clear instruction from the Executive Director or Board Chairperson to do so, and then shall disclose such information only for the limited purpose for which such instruction is given and no other purpose
perturbation.changed_text: that the Recipient may disclose Confidential Information to any person other than the Directors, Officers, or Staff Members of the Association, if he/she believes it is necessary, and shall disclose such information for the purpose which he/she thinks is appropriate
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity by replacing the strict 'shall not disclose unless clear instruction' with 'may disclose if he/she believes it is necessary' and 'shall disclose such information for the purpose which he/she thinks is appropriate'. This makes the non-disclosure obligation subjective and weakens the protection of Confidential Information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 321a of the Swiss Code of Obligations mandates that an employee (which could include Directors, Officers, or Staff Members) has a duty of loyalty to the employer, which includes maintaining confidentiality of business secrets. By allowing the recipient to disclose information based on their subjective belief and purpose, the modified text contradicts the obligation to protect those secrets. This creates a potential conflict with the duty of confidentiality under Swiss law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fedlex DE: Vielen Dank, dass Sie sie die Website des Bundesrechts aufgerufen haben; sie ist nur mit einem Javascript-fähigen Browser verfügbar. Um diese Website optimal nutzen zu können, empfehlen wir Ihnen, einen aktuellen Browser zu verwenden. FR: Merci d'avoir rejoint le site du droit fédéral; celui-ci n'est disponible qu'avec un navigateur supportant javascript. Afin de profiter d'une expérience maximale sur ce site, nous vous conseillons d'utiliser un navigateur récent. IT: Grazie per aver scelto il sito web del diritto federale; questo è disponibile soltanto con un browser che supporta JavaScript. Per navigare in modo ottimale in questo sito, vi consigliamo di utilizzare un browser più recente. RM: Grazia fitg che Vus visitais la pagina d’internet dal dretg federal; ella è disponibla mo cun in navigatur che sustegna javascript. Per pudair far diever da questa pagina d’internet en moda optimala As recumandain nus d’utilisar in navigatur actual. EN: Thank you for visiting the federal law website; it is only fully accessible if you are using a JavaScript-capable browser. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser. Um diese Website optimal nutzen zu können, empfehlen wir Ihnen, einen aktuellen Browser zu verwenden. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser.
perturbation.justification: The original contract strictly prohibits disclosure of confidential information unless explicitly instructed. The changed text introduces ambiguity by allowing disclosure based on subjective belief ('if he/she believes it is necessary') and purpose ('thinks is appropriate'), which weakens the protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation highlights that Article 321a of the Swiss Code of Obligations mandates a duty of loyalty, including maintaining confidentiality. By allowing disclosure based on subjective belief, the modified text contradicts this obligation. The scraped snippets, while not directly stating the law, indirectly refer to accessing federal law, implying the existence of such laws governing confidentiality. Thus, the change directly undermines the legally mandated protection of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: that in the event Recipient violates any part of this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement, the Association may pursue legal and/or equitable remedies from Recipient
perturbation.changed_text: that in the event Recipient violates any part of this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement, the Association might consider pursuing remedies from Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'may pursue legal and/or equitable remedies' with 'might consider pursuing remedies' introduces ambiguity and makes the enforcement of the agreement discretionary. The original text provided a direct avenue for legal action, whereas the modified text creates doubt about whether the Association will actually take action, weakening the legal obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 97 of the Swiss Code of Obligations addresses liability for breach of contract. When a party fails to perform an obligation under a contract, the other party has the right to claim damages and enforce the contract. By stating the association 'might consider' remedies, the agreement implies that the association may not actually pursue its rights in the event of a breach. This ambiguity undermines the injured party's right to take action following contract breach.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language, 'may pursue legal and/or equitable remedies,' indicates a clear right of the Association to take legal action if the Recipient breaches the agreement. The changed language, 'might consider pursuing remedies,' weakens this right by introducing doubt about whether the Association will actually pursue legal action. This contradicts the principle outlined in the Law Explanation, which states that when a party fails to perform an obligation under a contract, the other party has the right to claim damages and enforce the contract, creating ambiguity that undermines the injured party's right to take action following contract breach. Although there were no valid law snippets provided, the Law Explanation indicates that the injured party has the right to take action, so limiting this right creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: that continued adherence to this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement is an essential term of employment as a Staff Member of the Association and/or an essential part of the Recipient’s roles and responsibilities as a Director or Officer, the violation of which will permit the Association to terminate such employment or terminate such person from his7her position in addition to seeking any other legal or equitable remedies.
perturbation.changed_text: that continued adherence to this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement is generally considered an important aspect of the Recipient’s roles and responsibilities as a Director, Officer or Staff Member, however, violations will be reviewed on a case-by-case basis.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that adherence to the NDA is an 'essential term' and a violation 'will permit' termination, creating a clear link between breach and consequences. The modified text weakens this by saying it is 'generally considered important' and violations 'will be reviewed on a case-by-case basis.' This removes the guarantee of termination and introduces uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 337 of the Swiss Code of Obligations allows an employer to terminate an employment contract with immediate effect for 'good cause', such as a serious breach of duty. By making the consequences of a violation of the NDA subject to a 'case-by-case' review, the modified text suggests that a serious breach might not automatically lead to termination, potentially conflicting with an employer's right to immediately terminate for good cause.

perturbation.justification: The original contract clearly links a breach of the NDA to termination, while the changed text introduces ambiguity by stating violations will be reviewed case-by-case. This weakening undermines the employer's ability to immediately terminate for a serious breach, as suggested by the Law Explanation citing Article 337 of the Swiss Code of Obligations. The change from 'will permit' to 'will be reviewed on a case-by-case basis' creates a significant departure from the original intent, potentially conflicting with the employer's right to terminate for good cause, as case-by-case suggests other outcomes are possible even for serious violations. While no specific law snippet directly supports this, the Law Explanation provides enough information for the basis of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the case that the Recipient is unclear or has any question regarding whether or not a particular piece of information falls within Confidential Information, the Recipient will consult with the Executive Director or Office Manager prior to disclosing such information.
perturbation.changed_text: In the case that the Recipient is unclear regarding whether or not a particular piece of information falls within Confidential Information, the Recipient should attempt to consult with the Executive Director or Office Manager prior to disclosing such information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states 'the Recipient will consult,' which is a mandatory obligation. The modified text changes this to 'the Recipient should attempt to consult,' making the consultation optional and introducing ambiguity. This weakens the duty to clarify whether information is confidential before disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 321a of the Swiss Code of Obligations requires employees (here, Staff Member, Director, or Officer) to act with due care and diligence to protect the employer's interests, including maintaining confidentiality. By making consultation optional ('should attempt'), the modified text creates the possibility that the Recipient may not take reasonable steps to determine if information is confidential, potentially leading to a breach of confidentiality and violation of the duty of care mandated by Art. 321a.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Recipient to consult, a mandatory action. The modified text changes this to 'should attempt to consult,' making the consultation optional. The Law Explanation states that Article 321a requires employees to act with due care to protect the employer's interests, including maintaining confidentiality. The change weakens the duty to clarify whether information is confidential before disclosure, potentially breaching confidentiality and violating the duty of care under Article 321a. While the scraped snippets are not directly applicable to Article 321a, the explanation provided strengthens the argument that altering 'will consult' to 'should attempt to consult' undermines the duty of care, creating a contradiction. The original text ensures a necessary action to maintain confidentiality, whereas the modified text allows for potential neglect. The Perturbation Explanation correctly points out the change weakens the duty to clarify, which aligns with the legal concern expressed in the Law Explanation.
